skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create talking head and AI avatar videos with the belt CLI and hosted inference.sh video, image, TTS, and lipsync apps.
skill.summary: Generate AI presenter, lipsync, dubbing, and UGC-style avatar videos from portraits, scripts, and audio.
skill.author: skills-shell

security_audit.summary: Static command and network findings are mostly true positives because the skill teaches users to run belt CLI workflows against hosted inference apps. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential theft, or weak cryptography was found; the weak-crypto alert on SKILL.md line 3 is a false positive. Publish with a warning about external processing, shell execution, account costs, and local output files.

medium_findings.title: User-Triggered External CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for belt commands and provides many belt app run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it can spend account credits and process user media through external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools header and repeated belt examples confirm intentional command execution. The commands are documented user workflows, not hidden auto-execution or malware.

medium_findings.title: External Hosted Media Processing
medium_findings.description: The examples send portrait images, audio, scripts, and videos to inference.sh apps and related model providers. Users should avoid sensitive personal data unless they accept those service terms.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples pass remote URLs and text inputs to hosted inference apps. No covert endpoint or credential exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Local Output Files May Be Overwritten
low_findings.description: Several examples redirect command output to fixed JSON filenames. This is normal workflow storage, but running them may overwrite files with the same names.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Shell redirection to fixed filenames is visible in the examples. The impact is limited because the filenames are local workflow artifacts.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged SKILL.md line 3, but that line is a YAML description of avatar video features. No cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited line found only marketplace description text. There is no crypto code, weak hash, encryption mode, or key handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Triggered External CLI Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for belt commands and provides many belt app run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it can spend account credits and process user media through external services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools header and repeated belt examples confirm intentional command execution. The commands are documented user workflows, not hidden auto-execution or malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Hosted Media Processing
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples send portrait images, audio, scripts, and videos to inference.sh apps and related model providers. Users should avoid sensitive personal data unless they accept those service terms.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples pass remote URLs and text inputs to hosted inference apps. No covert endpoint or credential exfiltration evidence was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Output Files May Be Overwritten
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples redirect command output to fixed JSON filenames. This is normal workflow storage, but running them may overwrite files with the same names.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Shell redirection to fixed filenames is visible in the examples. The impact is limited because the filenames are local workflow artifacts.

content.user_title: Create AI Avatar Videos with Belt
content.value_statement: Producing avatar videos often requires separate tools for portraits, voices, lipsync, and rendering. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical belt CLI workflows for hosted avatar generation.
actual_capabilities: Shows belt CLI commands for AI avatar and talking head video generation.
Compares P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse lipsync workflows.
Provides text-to-avatar, audio-to-avatar, dubbing, and UGC-style examples.
Explains how to combine image generation, TTS, transcription, and lipsync apps.
Lists practical model choices for speed, cost, language support, and output quality.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI, an inference.sh account, and access to selected hosted apps.
Runs can send user images, audio, scripts, and videos to external services.
Costs, model availability, and output quality depend on provider settings.
The skill provides workflow guidance, not a local video generation engine.

use_cases.title: Create Product Presenter Clips
use_cases.description: Generate short avatar presenter videos for demos, ads, and launch announcements from a portrait and script.

use_cases.title: Localize Training Content
use_cases.description: Transcribe, translate, synthesize speech, and lipsync an existing video for another language.

use_cases.title: Prototype Character Dialogue
use_cases.description: Turn character art and voice lines into quick talking avatar clips for game or story testing.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Avatar Clip
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a simple talking head from one portrait and one script.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-avatar-video skill to make a 720p talking head video from my portrait URL and this short script: [script].

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Right Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare available avatar models before spending credits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse for a realistic presenter video with this source material.

prompt_templates.title: Marketing UGC Batch
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan several variations for a campaign.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a belt workflow for three UGC-style avatar videos with different voices, tones, and presenter backgrounds.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Dubbing Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert an existing video into another language.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a full belt workflow to transcribe this video, prepare translated speech, and lipsync the original speaker.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a fast avatar presenter video from a portrait and a 30 second script.
output: Recommends P-Video-Avatar for speed, cost, built-in TTS, and 720p or 1080p output.
Prepares the needed image URL, voice choice, language, script, and resolution fields.
Explains that the run will process the portrait and script through hosted inference services.

output_examples.input: A user wants to dub an existing product video into another language.
output: Breaks the workflow into transcription, translation, speech synthesis, and lipsync steps.
Suggests a TTS model for the new voice and LatentSync for syncing the original video.
Warns the user to review consent, privacy, and cost before submitting source media.

output_examples.input: A user needs several UGC-style clips with different presenters.
output: Plans reusable portrait, script, voice, tone, and video prompt variations.
Uses a batch-oriented belt workflow while keeping each generated clip distinct.
Recommends checking each result for brand safety, pronunciation, and visual artifacts.

best_practices: Use clear, front-facing portraits with good lighting and simple backgrounds.
Review provider terms before submitting personal images, voice data, or client media.
Start with short low-resolution tests before rendering final 1080p videos.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private likenesses, voices, or videos without consent.
Do not paste unescaped scripts directly into shell commands when they contain quotes.
Do not run expensive batch jobs before validating one short sample output.

faq.question: Does this skill generate videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides belt CLI runs that use hosted inference.sh apps and related providers.

faq.question: Which model should I try first?
faq.answer: The skill recommends P-Video-Avatar first because it includes TTS and focuses on speed and cost.

faq.question: Can I use my own recorded audio?
faq.answer: Yes. Several workflows accept an audio URL for lipsync or audio-driven avatar generation.

faq.question: Can it create UGC-style ad videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes presenter, voice, tone, and background prompts for UGC-style clips.

faq.question: What data leaves my machine?
faq.answer: Portraits, scripts, audio, and video URLs may be sent to hosted apps when you run belt commands.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive client media?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review consent, privacy requirements, provider terms, and retention policies first.
